---
title: "Loops and Tracing"
description: "Repeat work with for and while loops, control loop flow, and trace changing values."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Repeat work with for and while loops, control loop flow, and trace changing values.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Loops and Tracing** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 4 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **For Loops** — Repeat an action once for each item in a collection.
- Section 2: **While Loops** — Repeat while a condition remains true and stop safely.
- Section 3: **Loop Control** — Use break, continue, and clear stopping rules inside loops.
- Section 4: **Tracing Code** — Predict program behavior by tracking values line by line.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
A loop is a repeated path. The important debugging question is: what changes each
time around the loop?

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  start([Start loop]) --> check{"Another item<br/>or condition true?"}
  check -- Yes --> bind["Set loop variable<br/>or inspect condition"]
  bind --> body["Run loop body"]
  body --> update["Update state<br/>total, count, index, etc."]
  update --> check
  check -- No --> done([Continue after loop])
```

Use a trace table beside this diagram: one row for each trip through the loop.
:::

## Section 1: For Loops

Repeat an action once for each item in a collection.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A for loop is like handing papers to every student in a row: do the same action for each
person.
:::

### Mental model

A for loop visits items one at a time and runs the same block for each item.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
names = ["Ada", "Grace", "Katherine"]
for name in names:
    print(f"Hello, {name}")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `names = ["Ada", "Grace", "Katherine"]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `for name in names:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(f"Hello, {name}")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
The loop variable changes on each iteration. Do not assume it keeps the first value.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-for-loops",
  "title": "For Loops checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes For Loops?",
      "options": [
        "A for loop visits items one at a time and runs the same block for each item.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A for loop visits items one at a time and runs the same block for each item."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A for loop is like handing papers to every student in a row: do the same action for each person."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: While Loops

Repeat while a condition remains true and stop safely.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A while loop is like filling a glass until it reaches a line: check the condition before
each pour.
:::

### Mental model

A while loop keeps going until its condition becomes false.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
count = 3
while count > 0:
    print(count)
    count = count - 1
print("go")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `count = 3` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `while count > 0:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(count)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `count = count - 1` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `print("go")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Forgetting to update the condition can create an infinite loop. Make the stopping rule
visible.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-while-loops",
  "title": "While Loops checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes While Loops?",
      "options": [
        "A while loop keeps going until its condition becomes false.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A while loop keeps going until its condition becomes false."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A while loop is like filling a glass until it reaches a line: check the condition before each pour."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: Loop Control

Use break, continue, and clear stopping rules inside loops.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Loop control is like a teacher pausing a line: skip one step, stop early, or continue
normally depending on what happens.
:::

### Mental model

Loop control changes the normal path through a loop when a special situation appears.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
for number in range(1, 6):
    if number == 3:
        continue
    if number == 5:
        break
    print(number)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `for number in range(1, 6):` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `if number == 3:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `continue` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `if number == 5:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `break` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
6. Continue the same process for the remaining lines, one line at a time.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Using break too early can hide later data. Explain the reason for early exit with clear
variable names or comments.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-loop-control",
  "title": "Loop Control checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Loop Control?",
      "options": [
        "Loop control changes the normal path through a loop when a special situation appears.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Loop control changes the normal path through a loop when a special situation appears."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Loop control is like a teacher pausing a line: skip one step, stop early, or continue normally depending on what happens."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 4: Tracing Code

Predict program behavior by tracking values line by line.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Tracing is like following a package through a delivery route: note where it starts, each
stop, and where it ends.
:::

### Mental model

Tracing means writing down how variables change as Python executes each line.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
total = 0
for number in [2, 4, 6]:
    total = total + number
    print(total)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `total = 0` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `for number in [2, 4, 6]:` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `total = total + number` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(total)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Skipping lines when tracing causes wrong predictions. Move one line at a time like
Python does.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "core-python-tracing-code",
  "title": "Tracing Code checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Tracing Code?",
      "options": [
        "Tracing means writing down how variables change as Python executes each line.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Tracing means writing down how variables change as Python executes each line."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Tracing is like following a package through a delivery route: note where it starts, each stop, and where it ends."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **For Loops**: A for loop visits items one at a time and runs the same block for each item.
- **While Loops**: A while loop keeps going until its condition becomes false.
- **Loop Control**: Loop control changes the normal path through a loop when a special situation appears.
- **Tracing Code**: Tracing means writing down how variables change as Python executes each line.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Built-in types: https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
